content.problemStatement:
Recently Chef learned about Longest Increasing Subsequence. To be precise, he means longest **strictly** increasing subsequence, when he talks of longest increasing subsequence.  To check his understanding, he took his favorite **n**-digit number and for each of its **n** digits, he computed the length of the longest increasing subsequence of digits ending with that digit. Then he stored these lengths in an array named **LIS**.

For example, let us say that Chef's favourite **4**-digit number is **1531**, then the **LIS** array would be **[1, 2, 2, 1]**. The length of longest increasing subsequence ending at first digit is **1** (the digit **1** itself) and at the second digit is **2** (**[1, 5]**), at third digit is also **2** (**[1, 3]**), and at the **4**th digit is **1** (the digit **1** itself).

Now Chef is wondering, how many different **n**-digit numbers (without leading zeros) are there that have exactly the given **LIS** array? As this number could be very large, output it modulo **(109 + 7)**.
constraints: Sum of n over all T testcases is denoted by S
It is guaranteed that at least one n-digit number having the given LIS array exists.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer n denoting the number of digits in Chef's favourite number. The second line will contain n space separated integers denoting LIS array, i.e. LIS1, LIS2, ..., LISn.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer — count of different n-digit numbers having the given LIS array, modulo (10^9 + 7).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1. All one-digit numbers have the same LIS array, so the answer is 10: 0, 1, 2, 3, ..., 9.
Example cases 2 & 3. Overall we have 90 two-digit numbers (from 10 to 99). Numbers with second digit strictly greater than first digit have LIS array [1, 2] and there are 36 such numbers. All the other two-digit numbers have LIS array [1, 1].

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to calculate the number of ways to form each digit of the number given the LIS array. We start by initializing a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the number of ways to form the first i digits of the number such that the longest increasing subsequence ending at the ith digit is j. We then fill up the dp array by iterating over each digit and each possible value of the longest increasing subsequence. Finally, we return the sum of dp[n][j] for all j from 1 to n, modulo (10^9 + 7).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n^2) — where T is the number of test cases and n is the maximum number of digits in the input.
